2605.21363.title: "I didn't Make the Micro Decisions": Measuring, Inducing, and Exposing Goal-Level AI Contributions in Collaboration
2605.21363.paper_summary: As large language models (LLMs) increasingly shape how users form, refine, and extend their goals, attributing contributions in human-AI collaboration becomes critical for users calibrating their own reliance and for evaluators assessing AI-assisted work. Yet existing methods focus on final artifacts, missing the process through which goals themselves are jointly shaped. We introduce a goal-level attribution framework, CoTrace, that decomposes explicit goals into verifiable requirements and traces both direct contributions and indirect influences across dialogue turns. Applying CoTrace to 638 real-world collaboration logs, we find that while models account for only 11-26% of goal-shaping contribution, they contribute substantially more on introducing lower-level concrete requirements, and make various kinds of indirect contributions. Through controlled simulations, we show that interaction design choices significantly affect model goal-shaping behavior. In a user study, exposing participants to goal-level analyses shifts their perceived contributions by nearly 2 points on a 5-point scale, revealing systematic miscalibration in how users understand their own AI-assisted work.
2605.21363.paper_summary_zh: 隨著大型語言模型（LLMs）越來越多地影響用戶如何形成、完善和擴展他們的目標，在人類與人工智慧的合作中歸因貢獻變得至關重要，這對於用戶調整自己的依賴程度以及評估者評估AI輔助工作而言都是如此。<lb/>然而，現有的方法專注於最終產物，忽略了目標本身是如何共同形成的過程。<lb/>我們提出了一個目標層級的歸因框架CoTrace，它將明確的目標分解為可驗證的需求，並追蹤對話回合中直接貢獻和間接影響。<lb/>將CoTrace應用於638個現實世界的合作日誌，我們發現，儘管模型僅占目標塑造貢獻的11-26%，但它們在引入更低層次具體需求方面的貢獻要大得多，並且還做出各種間接貢獻。<lb/>通過控制模擬，我們顯示互動設計選擇顯著影響模型的目標塑造行為。<lb/>在一項用戶研究中，讓參與者接觸到目標層級的分析，使他們對貢獻的感知在5分制上轉變了近2分，揭示了用戶對自己AI輔助工作的理解存在系統性錯誤校準。
2605.21363.author: Eunsu Kim et.al.
2605.21363.authors: Eunsu Kim, Jessica R. Mindel, Kyungjin Kim, Sherry Tongshuang Wu
